title: Fixing a dripping tap: compression and ceramic disc types
subtitle: Diagnosing whether to replace a washer, a cartridge, or repack a gland, with the water off at the supply
excerpt: A dripping tap wastes 3 to 24 litres per day depending on drip rate and mains pressure. Over a year, a slow drip wastes over 1,000 litres. The repair is straightforward for both traditional compression taps (washer replacement) and modern ceramic disc taps (cartridge replacement). This guide covers diagnosing the type, isolating the water, and completing the repair without a plumber.
sourceNotes: WRAS guidance on domestic tap maintenance. BSI BS 1010 on draw-off taps for use with drinking water supplies. Plumbers Mate technical guidance on compression vs ceramic disc tap types. Which? guide to fixing dripping taps. MCS approved contractor guidance on water waste reduction.

glossaryTerms.definition: A traditional tap design where a rubber washer is pressed against a valve seat by tightening the tap handle. Dripping is caused by a worn or deformed washer. Repair involves replacing the washer.

A modern tap design using two ceramic discs that rotate relative to each other to control flow. The discs are very hard and resist wear. Dripping is usually caused by debris or a worn O-ring in the cartridge. Repair involves replacing the cartridge.

content.text: A drip from the spout of a
compression tap
means the washer is worn. A drip from the spout of a
ceramic disc tap
means the cartridge has debris or a worn seal. A drip from the base of the tap handle or around the packing nut on a compression tap is a gland seal problem. Each has a different repair.
What you'll need
A replacement washer (13 mm or 19 mm for most UK taps; take the old one to the hardware shop to match).
A replacement cartridge for a ceramic disc tap. Photograph the old cartridge or note the tap brand before ordering.
PTFE tape for re-sealing the headgear thread.
A bucket and towels for the residual water.
The repair: compression tap
Turn off the water at the local isolation valve under the basin, or at the mains stopcock. Open the tap to drain the pressure.
Remove the tap handle: prise off the decorative cap on top, undo the screw below it, and lift the handle off.
Undo the packing nut (hexagonal brass fitting below the handle). Use the adjustable spanner with a cloth to protect the finish.
Unscrew the headgear (the whole internal assembly). The washer is at the bottom of the headgear, held by a small nut. Replace the washer. Some headgear accepts a snap-fit washer.
Wrap 2 to 3 turns of PTFE tape around the headgear thread. Reassemble in reverse order. Turn on the water slowly.
The repair: ceramic disc tap
Isolate the water and drain as above.
Remove the handle and unscrew the cartridge retaining ring. The ceramic cartridge lifts straight out.
Inspect the cartridge seats and O-rings. If visible grit or a broken O-ring is present, clean or replace. If the ceramics are chipped or cracked, replace the whole cartridge.
Fit the new cartridge in the same orientation (note the slot position before removing). Reassemble and test.

attrs.symptom: Tap still drips after washer replacement

content.text: The valve seat (the brass seat inside the tap body against which the washer presses) is scored or corroded. A valve seat grinding tool can resurface the seat; alternatively a plumber replaces the tap body.
